findings.title: EmotionState alias comment claims backwards-compatibility that does not exist
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * Backwards-compatible alias for use in response-generator and proactive-trigger.
 * Maps to the same 6 persona emotion states.
 */
export type EmotionState = PersonaEmotion
findings.description: The comment says 'Backwards-compatible alias' but the underlying values changed entirely in this diff: the previous EmotionState was 'neutral' | 'happy' | 'caring' | 'playful' | 'worried' | 'sad', while PersonaEmotion is 'idle' | 'curious' | 'caring' | 'worried' | 'sleepy' | 'excited'. Only 'caring' and 'worried' survived. This is a breaking rename, not backwards compatibility. The comment will mislead future maintainers into thinking existing consumers of EmotionState are unaffected.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite to: 'Type alias so response-generator and proactive-trigger can refer to emotion states without importing PersonaEmotion directly. Note: the emotion values were renamed in this iteration (idle replaces neutral, curious replaces happy, etc.).'

findings.title: AnimaEmotionPayload JSDoc claims it matches stage-ui EmotionPayload but types differ
findings.description: The comment says this interface 'matching the stage-ui EmotionPayload interface', but stage-ui's EmotionPayload defines name as type Emotion (an enum), not string. AnimaEmotionPayload.name is 'string' which is a wider type. This mismatch means the comment overstates the contract alignment -- a future maintainer might assume these are interchangeable when they are not structurally identical.
findings.suggestion: Either change the comment to 'Loosely models the stage-ui EmotionPayload. name is kept as string to avoid a direct dependency on stage-ui.' to explain the deliberate widening, or import and use the Emotion enum type directly.

findings.title: Response generator comment references 'initial integration' scope -- will rot
findings.code_snippet: * Uses template-based generation to avoid LLM dependency during initial integration.
findings.description: This comment ties itself to a temporal scope ('during initial integration'). Once LLM-based generation is added (or if it never is), this comment will either be stale or misleading. This matches the walking skeleton scope annotation pattern common in this codebase.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite to describe the current approach without referencing the timeline: 'Uses template-based generation. No LLM dependency required.' If LLM generation is a planned evolution, that belongs in a design doc, not inline.

findings.title: Hardcoded count 'Three preset persona templates' will rot if templates change
findings.description: The comment says 'Three preset persona templates' but the count is a detail that will become stale as soon as a template is added or removed. The array itself is self-documenting for its length.
findings.suggestion: Remove the count: 'Preset persona templates representing different character archetypes.'

positive_observations: All exported functions and interfaces have JSDoc with accurate @param and @returns documentation
The emotion state machine comment on lines 19-24 explains the state transition model concisely, providing valuable context beyond what the code alone shows
TriggerInput JSDoc on types.ts line 60 includes a good 'why' comment: 'Intentionally decoupled from context-engine's ActivityContext to keep persona-engine independent'
The emotion-bridge.ts comment listing Anima Emotion enum values is factually accurate and helps readers understand the mapping without needing to look up stage-ui
